verses.english: Now, the teachings of yoga are presented since the student is ready to received these teachings.
The purpose of yoga is to quieten the fluctuations of the mind.
Once the state of Yoga is achieved, pure consciousness resides in its intrinsic nature.
Till the state of Yoga is achieved, consciousness identifies itself with the mental patterns.
These mental patterns can be classified into five types, which include both harmful and harmless ones.
These five types of mental patters are right perception, misperception, conceptualization, deep sleep, and memory.
Right perception arises from direct observation, inference, or the words of others.
False knowledge arises from misperception, which is not based on reality.
Imagination is a product of language-based knowledge, rather than direct experience with the physical world.
The pattern of deep sleep is rooted in the perception that everything ceases to exist.
'Remembering' refers to the act of retaining past experiences in memory.
In order to quiet the fluctuations of the mind, one needs - practice and dispassion.
Practice involves the persistent effort to abide in 'elevated' state.
Skillful and continuous cultivation of this 'practice' with devotion establishes firm roots over time.
Complete dispassion occurs when one no longer becomes attached to anything, whether perceived (seen & heard) directly or learned.
Pure awareness can recognize its independence from the fundamental qualities of nature when ultimate dispassion is achieved.
Initially, the process of stilling the mind involves four levels of elevated cognition, including analytical thinking, insight, bliss, and the sense of self.
As one practices steadily to bring all thoughts to a standstill, these four types of cognition gradually disappear, leaving only latent impressions in the subconscious mind.
Once the body is gone and these latent impressions might give rise to rebirth.
For others, the path to realization involves faith, energy, right cognition in form of memory and to understand the various elevated levels of wisdom.
Those who earnestly seek liberation will find that realization is within reach, depending on the intensity of their practice.
The proximity to realization varies according to whether the practice is mild, moderate, or intense.
Another pathway to realization involves being directed toward the ideal of pure awareness i.e. Ishvara or God.
Ishvara or God is a unique and incorruptible manifestation of pure awareness that is completely independent of cause and effect, and devoid of any latent impressions.
Isvara or God is limitless and with unparalleled omniscience.
Being beyond time, Isvara or God is revered as the primoridial master by the ancients.
Isvara or God is symbolized by the sacred sound of "Om".
This verbatim of Ishvara is to be repeated with devotion along with its meaning.
As one continues to focus on Ishvara as mentioned, one's consciouness ascends and also obstacles begin to dissipate.
Obstacles such as sickness, apathy, doubt, carelessness, laziness, hedonism, delusion, lack of progress, and inconstancy all serve as distractions that clouds consciousness and act as barriers.
These distractions may result in feelings of distress, depression, or an inability to maintain steadiness of posture or breathing (uncontrolled inhalations & exhalations).
However, focus towards one unwavering 'element' (here Ishwara) can help one subdue these distractions.
Other means involves radiating friendliness, compassion, delight, and equanimity towards one who is pleasant, in pain, good and bad respectively.
Another way involves pausing after forceful exhalations.
Steadily observing as new (sattvik) sensations arise  (by the means of any of the 5 sense organs) can be a way to subdue distractions and focus the mind.
Experiencing thoughts that are luminous and free of sorrow can also aid in achieving stillness of mind.
Focusing on things/people that do not inspire attachment can be a helpful technique in meditation.
Reflecting on insights gained while asleep and dreaming can be a source of guidance too.
Meditative absorption in any desired (sattvik) object of one's choice can lead to a state of deep concentration and awareness.
Complete absorption in any object, whether subtlest and (macroscopic or) microscopic , is possible through meditation.

As the fluctuations of mind fade away, a transparent mode of perception called 'samapatti'* alike to a transparent jewel/prism takes over. This transparent mind reflects everything in its perview equally - subject, object, or act of perceiving - like a prism (reflecting the constituents of light in seven colors).
In the state of 'savitarka samapatti'* i.e. samapatti* with physical awareness constitutes with liguistic idea of word, meaning and idea.
At the next stage, objects lose their association with memory and are formless, revealing only their essential nature. This stage is known as 'nirvitarka'*.

verses.sanskrit: एतयैव सविचारा निर्विचारा च सूक्ष्मविषया व्याख्याता
verses.english: Similarly, the states of 'savichara'* and 'nirvichara'* have objects of contemplation which are subtle in nature.
By tracing subtle objects back to their undifferentiated nature, they can be understood.
The four atages of awareness (mentioned above) - savitarka*, nirvitarka*, savichara* and nirvichara *- are referred to as meditative absorption that contains latent impressions (seeds).
(Among these four stages) once the stage of nirvichara* is comprehended, the nature of the self is illuminated.
The wisdom that arises from this comprehension is infallible.
This wisdom, unlike knowledge gained from inference or instruction, therefore holds a special importance.
The impressions/ideas generated by this special knowledge, hinder the activation of other impressions.
When these impressions too come to an end, and the movement of consciousness is entirely pacified, this stage of 'impressionless' consciousness is termed as 'seedless' meditative absorption.
Kriya Yoga comprises of self discipline, study of self, and devotion towards Ishvara.
The aim of this path of action is to eliminate the roots of suffering and accomplish samadhi* (an advanced meditative state).
The roots of suffering include - ignorance, the sense of 'I', attachment, aversion, and fear of death.
The causes of suffering germinate due to 'ignorance' and these sufferings can be either dormant, weakened, interrupted or fully activated.
Ignorance is the state where one confuses impermanence, impurity, distress, and non self with permanence, purity, happiness, and self.
The sense of 'I' (wrongly) identifies perception of pure awareness as the perception of the senses.
Attachment is the cause of pleasurable experiences.
Aversion is a cause of suffering(s).
Even the wise similar to a non-wise, instinctively fear death, perpetuating suffering.
These kleshas* (sufferings) are very subtle (hence not easily erased). They are eliminated only when the mind merges into its orgin.
Through meditation, the fluctuations of mind that arise due to these kleshas* (sufferings) can be terminated.
The deposits of karma* (actions) are rooted in the causes of klesha* (suffering), which is experienced either in the future or in the present.
As long as the root source i.e. klesha (suffering) exists, its contents will continue to result as - type of birth, span of life, and experience of life.
Each person's- type of birth, span of life, and experience of life, fructifes either as pleasure or pain, which is resultant of their past virtuous or viceful actions.

verses.sanskrit: परिणाम तापसंस्कार दुःखैर्गुणवृत्तिविरोधाच्च दुःखमेव सर्वं विवेकिनः
verses.english: The wise recognize that suffering is inherent in all experiences, whether it be the agony of the results of actions, or the pain of distress, or of the burden of latent impressions (samskaras*) filled with suffering, along with the constant struggle of the flutuations of the mind due to the fundamental qualities (gunas*) of nature.
However, it is possible to prevent suffering from arising altogether.
The root cause of all suffering is the mistaken belief that pure awareness and the perception of awareness are inseparable.

The phenomenal world that awareness perceives is characterized by luminosity (sattva), activity (rajas), and inertia (tamas), and is composed of - elements and senses, and serves the purpose of both sensual experience as well as liberation (to drasta*).
The various levels of existence - whether they are  differentiated, undifferentiated, distinct or indistinct - are all expressions of the basic characteristics inherent in nature (gunas*).
Pure awareness (drashta*) is simply the act of seeing (hence called seer), but it typically appears to function through the perceiving mind despite its purity.
The purpose of the phenomenal world (drashya*) is essentially to reveal this truth (for the seer).
Once this realization occurs, the phenomenal world ceases to be for them, but remains a common reality for the (non-realized) others.
It is the apparent indivisibility of pure awareness and the phenomenal world (i.e. the concept of conjunction), that gives insight into the potency of both the possessor and the possessed.
The cause of this phenomenon (of conjunction) is ignorance.
When this ignorance fades, the appearance of indivisibility also fades, and it becomes clear that pure awareness is free and unaffected by phenomenal world which the state of liberation (kaivalya*).

Unflinching discriminative discernment (between the seer and the seen) is the path to liberation (not the final state yet, this is the upper stage of Ritambhara pragya (1.48) where even this prayga ceases).
On this path of liberation, the one (yogi) with the wisdom of discriminative discernment extends to seven ultimate stages.
By practicing the components of yoga, impurities diminish, allowing the light of understanding to shine and illuminate the path to discriminative awareness.
Social discipline, personal observances, pyscho-physical posture, breath regulation, withdrawl of senses, concentration, meditation and liberation make up the eight limbs of yoga.
The five social disciplines are non-violence, being truthful, not stealing, practicing celibacy, and avoiding materialism.
These principles are solemn resolutions. They are not bound by one's social status, territory, specific time or any duration. They are universal.
The five personal observances are purifying the body, cultivating contentment, practicing austerity, self-study, and devotion to God.
Negative thoughts can be countered by developing positive ones.

verses.sanskrit: वितर्का हिंसादयः कृतकारितानुमोदिता लोभक्रोधमोहपूर्वका मृदुमध्याधिमात्रा दुःखाज्ञानानन्तफला इति प्रतिपक्षभावनम्
verses.english: Negative thoughts such as violence etc. arise from greed, anger, or attachment, and regardless of their intensity such as less, medium or excessive, inevitably lead to ignorance and suffering. Therefore, it is necessary to cultivate counteracting (i.e.positive) thoughts.
When one is strongly rooted in non-violence, it fosters an environment (around that yogi) where others resign their hatred.
Those who become firmly rooted in truthfulness, experience every action and its outcome in accordance with the truth.
For those established in the virtue of non-stealing (or not accepting which is not their's), entire treasures are revealed.
Celibacy when deeply ingrained secures energy.
Being resolute in the state of non-greediness (desirelessness), the understanding of (previous) births is revealed.
Practicing cleanliness, one becomes free from the body's compulsions and from the need of (physically) associating with others.
Developing sattva through the practice of cleanliness (mental purification) leads to radiance, increased ability to focus, sense control, and ability for self-awareness.
Contentment brings an unmatched sense of joy.
Through intense discipline, impurities are burned away, and the body and senses become highly refined.
Self-reflection strengthens the connection with one's personal deity.
Devotion towards God or Ishvara* bestows the competence to attain samadhi* (liberation).
The body should actualize both steadiness and ease during a psychophysical posture.
This is possible when all effort is relaxed, and a sense of unity between the body and the infinite universe emerges.
At this point, the dualities of opposites no longer disturbs one.
After the psychophysical posture is achieved, breath regulation follows. It happens when inhalations and exhalations are regulated.

(Breath regulation or Pranayama* has three dimensions) inhalation, exhalation, and the (various) pauses (kumbhakka*). By paying attention to the place, time and number of each aspects, they become more subtle and extended.
In the fourth dimension (of breath regulation), the division between inhaling and exhaling disappears.
Then, the concealer of the illumination (sattva*) fades.
Also, the capability of the mind to concentrate (dharana*) is actualized.
When the senses are disengaged from their respective sense objects and identifies with the nature of the chitta, then sense withdrawal (Pratyahara*) happens.
Afterwards, the supreme mastery over the senses is attained.
Concentration/Attention is focusing on a single place.
In meditation, one's entire perceptual experience becomes uninturreptedly aligned with that object.
When only the essence of that object (used for dhyana) remains, and (chitta's) own functions (other cognitive abilities) appears non-existent then samadhi* has been achieved.
The pure state of cognition (samyama*) involves all the (above) three (concentration, meditative absorption, and integration towards a single object.)
When one masters this discipline, light of wisdom dawns. [Stages of this wisdom has been mentioned in sutra 2.27]
It (Samyama* or pure state of cognition) should be applied (sequentially) on various stages or planes (of refined cognition).
These three limbs (concentration, absorption, and integration) are innermost with respect to the previous five.
However, even these three are considered extrinsic with respect to the state of integration that bears no potential/seeds (Nirbeej Samadhi*).

The cyclical suppression of worldly or distracting smaskaras and emergence of restraining (nirodha*) samskaras eventually fades the former resulting in the frequent contact of the chitta with restraining samskaras. This enables the state of restraint or Nirodha-parinama*.
The unhinged fludity of the thoughts in the mind is due to the samskaras.
In the state of liberation (Samadhi parinama*), the chitta eliminates multi-pointedness and develops one-pointedness.
Then, again, the one object or idea of focus present/arising on the chitta is identical to the one which subsides. This state is of one-pointedness (Ekagrata parinama*).

Similar to this (like the above changes in consiousness), dynamic changes (due to interplay of gunas) in material objects and senses can be interpreted at three levels : funtion (dharma*), timespan (lakshana*) and condition (awastha*).
The underlying foundation (dharmi*) remains the same, irrespective of whether it manifests in a particular form or funtion (dharma*) - before, during, or after the transformation.
These transformations (of objects/awareness) seem to occur in a specific manner, which is caused by change in sequence (of characteristics).
By carefully observing (a pure state of cognition or samyama*) the three aspects of change - function, timespan, and condition, one can gain insights into both the past and future.

The concepts of word, meaning, and perception are often intertwined, creating confusion between them. However, by paying attention to the differences between them through perfect discipline (samyama*), one can gain insights into the sounds of all living beings.
Examining the dormant mental impressions (samskaras*) through direct perception bestows insights into past lives.
It is possible to gain insight into the consciousness of another person by focusing on their perceptions (pratyaya*).
However, this insight does not extend to the object (alambana*) of their perceptions since it (the object) is not actually the subject matter of the one who is focussing (i.e. the yogi).
By observing the body's physical form with perfect discipline (samyama*), it becomes invisible. This happens when the power to perceive is suspended amidst the eye and the incoming light.

verses.sanskrit: सोपक्रमं निरुपक्रमं च कर्म तत्संयमादपरान्तज्ञानमरिष्टेभ्यो वा
verses.english: The consequences of actions (karma) may manifest instantly or take time to appear. Observing one's actions with perfect discipline or studying omens can offer insight into death.
By using perfect discipline to focus on friendliness etc.(i.e compassion, delight, and equanimity), one can internalize (their) strengths.
Through focusing (samyama*) on strengths, it is possible to acquire the powers of an elephant, etc.
Being deeply engaged in the radiance of the mind provides insight into the subtle, concealed, and remote aspects of existence (of things).
With perfect focusing (samayama*) on the sun, allows one to gain insight into the universe.
By using perfect discipline (samayama*) to focus on the moon, one can gain insight into the position of the stars.
Perfect discipline (samayama*) enables one to gain insight into the movements/speed of the stars by focusing on the polestar.
By using perfect discipline (samayama*) to focus on the navel plexus, one can gain insight into the organization of the body.
Focusing with perfect discipline (samayama*) on the pit of the throat helps to eliminate hunger and thirst.
Focusing with perfect discipline (samayama*) on the 'tortoise channel' (kurma naadi) helps to cultivate steadiness.
Through perfect discipline (samayama*), focusing on the light at the crown of the head allows one to acquire the perspective of those who have achieved perfection (siddhas).
Or, intuition (a foreground of districrinatory knowledge) may bring about knowlede of all.
By concentrating with utmost discipline on the heart, one can comprehend the essence of consciousness.

Wordly perception comprises of experiences where the pure consiousness (purusha*) is misconceived as pure (sattva*) intellect, whereas both are seperate. However, wordly perception exists to serve the comprehension of the other (i.e. pure consiousness).  By concentrating with utmost discipline on the one that dwells for itself, understanding of the pure consciousness is revealed.
As a result of this (above) understanding, intuition and elevated sense of hearing, touch, sight, taste, smell emerge.
Although these heightened sensory abilities may seem like accomplishments to outwardly directed mind, they are obstacles in the path of liberation/integration.
By letting go of one's attachment(s) and being acutely attuned to the pathways of consciousness, it can settle in another individual's body.

By achieving mastery over the vital energy in the neck and head (udana*), one can traverse water, mud, thorns, and other obstacles without making contact with the ground, but instead floating over them.
By acheiving mastery over the flow of energy through the solar plexus (samana*), one becomes radiant.
By intently focusing (samyama*) on the inter-relationship of the organ of hearing and space, one can attain divine hearing.
By intently focusing (samyama*) on the body's connection with the space and cultivating intense concentration (samapatti*) on the lightness as of cotton, one can travel through space.
The state of 'great disembodiment' (mahavidheha*) happens when the mind is projected outside the body into a non-imagined state. Then the veil obscuring the mind's radiance is lifted.

By intently observing the different dimensions of matter such as gross, intrinsic, subtle, interconnectedness (at the level of gunas) and purpose (of objects) with samyama*, one attains mastery over the elements.

As a result, extraordinary abilities, such as the power to reduce oneself to the size of an atom (anima*) etc. ( and 7 more abilities) manifest. Thus the body develops ability to transcend the laws of the physical matter and attains perfection too.
This level of perfection of the body includes qualities such as beauty, grace, strength, and the indestructibility of a thunderbolt.

One can gain control over sense organs by samyama* (on the various aspects of sense organs) - the process of obtaining knowledge, basic properties (of the sense organs), the individuating principle (asmita*), the inherent qualities and need (of the gunas*).
By doing that (samyama over sense organs) one also accquires ability to travel with the speed of thought, body independant perception and control over fundamental facets of matter (mula prakriti*).
Only when an individual perceives the distinction between pure awareness and the the intellect, they become knower of all and gain authority over existence.
When one detaches even from this omniscience and mastery, and the seeds of suffering decay, and pristine consciousness realizes its independent existence (state of kaivalya*).
Even if the divine beings call upon one, it is vital to refrain from becoming attached or arrogant; otherwise, suffering will resurface.
By intently focusing (samyama*) on the succession of moments in time, one gains insights that arise from discernment.
Due to which, distinction even between seemingly similar things on the basis of species, properties and location can be ascertained.

verses.sanskrit: तारकं सर्वविषयं सर्वथाविषयमक्रमं चेति विवेकजं ज्ञानम्
verses.english: This liberating insight deconstructs each object and every conditions in an time independant manner (i.e it is never changing unlike any other knowledge) and signifies viveka jnanam* or liberative discernment.
The state of 'kaivalya' or liberation dawns when the purity of intellect equals to that of the pure consciousness.
The mystic powers can manifest by the virtue from birth, herbal remedies, chanting of mantras, austerities and absorption in samadhi.
The transformation (or evolution) of a species is a result of nature's abundance. OR The transition to a new form occurs when the natural energies overflow.
The transformation from one form to another is not caused by the inherent factors, but they only serve as guidance, akin to a farmer (directing water flow for irrigation).
Assembly of chitta (mind) is done by ego only.
A sequence of consciousnesses that create a multitude of unique perceptions is a orchestrated by a single personal consciousness (of the yogi).
Consciousness focused due to meditative absorption does not add to the accumulation of potential impressions (unlike the other four means mentioned in 4.1).
The actions of a (true) yogi is neither white (right) nor black (wrong) unlike others (non-yogi) which are of three types (right, wrong or both).
From it (the three fold actions), furthur outcome in accordance with the latent subconscious traits manifest .

Memory and samskaras (subconscious impressions) are essentially identical, therefore, even if (both are) seperated by the virtue of birth, place and time they manifest without any difference of order.
These (subconscious impressions) are beginningless as the desire (to exist) is everlasting.
(The fourfold pillars that sustains samskara i.e.) Cause, Motive, Substratum, and Object (of impression) are interconnected, thus, it (samskara) becomes non-existent when these (four) do so.
Time periods of past and future are inherent in the underlying foundation (dharmi* refer 3.14), representing distinct aspects in the same continuum of experiential substances (dharma*).
Each of these (past, present or future), whether manifest or subtle, displays characteristics that stem from the fundamental qualities of nature (tri-gunas).
The uniformity in/of the transformation is due to the singularity in the elemental quality (or principles).
Because there is a multiplicity of minds (perceiving an object) but yet the object remains consistent, there is a difference in nature between the object and the mind (of the observer).

verses.sanskrit: न चैकचित्ततन्त्रं वस्तु तदप्रमाणकं तदा किं स्यात्
verses.english: An object is not dependant on a single mind (for its exixtence); if it were, then what happens to it when it is not perceived (by that particular mind)?
A thing is either known or not known by the mind depending on whether it is noticed by the mind.
The permutations of the mind are always known its master, the purusa soul, because of the soul's unchanging nature.
Nor is the mind self-illuminating, because of its nature as the object of perception.
There cannot be discernment of both (the mind and the object it perceives) at the same time.
If (the mind) were cognized by another mind, then there would be an infinite regress of one intelligence (being known) by another intelligence. Moreover, there would also be confusion of memory.
Although it is unchanging, consciousness becomes aware of its own intelligence by means of pervading the forms assumed by the intelligence.
The mind, colored by the seer as well as by that which is seen, knows all objects.
That mind, with its countless variegated subliminal impressions, exists for another entity (other than itself) because it operates in conjunction (with other instruments).
For one who sees the distinction (between the mind and the soul), reflecting on the nature of the self ceases.
At that point, the mind, inclined towards discrimination, gravitates toward ultimate liberation.
During the intervals (in this state of discriminate awareness) other ideas (arise) because of previous samskaras.
The removal (of these previous samskaras) is said to be like (the removal) of the klesa afflictions.
For one who has no interest even in (the fruits) of meditative wisdom on account of the highest degree of discriminative insight, the samadhi called dharma-megha, cloud of virtue, ensues.
From this comes the cessation of the klesas (impediments to yoga) and karma.
At this point, because of the unlimited nature of knowledge when all impurities have been removed from it, that which remains to be known is little.
As a result, there is a cessation of the ongoing permutations of the gunas, their purpose now fulfilled.
The progression (of any object through Time) corresponds to a (series of) moments. It is perceivable at the final (moment) of change.

verses.sanskrit: पुरुषार्थशून्यानां गुणानां प्रतिप्रसवः कैवल्यं स्वरूपप्रतिष्ठा वा चितिशक्तिरिति
verses.english: Ultimate liberation is when the gunas, devoid of any purpose for the purusa, return to their original (latent) state; in other words, when the power of consciousness is situated in its own essential nature.
